Case Study 01 of 03

Designing a Conversational
Assistant for Hearing Aid Support

Users were failing to get the help they needed — not because the technology was broken, but because the experience of asking for help was. The assistant existed, but the words, flows, and logic were not built around how real people describe a problem. I redesigned the entire content experience from the ground up.

My Role

UX Content Design
Conversation Flow Design
Microcopy and Tone
Interaction Logic Structuring

Tools

Figma
Phrase TMS
SharePoint
DITA XML

Context

Global hearing care brand
Working student role
Client name redacted

The Problem

Users did not know how to describe their problem — and the system was not helping them try.

When users experienced a hearing issue — sound too loud in crowds, voices sounding unnatural, difficulty following a conversation — they had no clear way to communicate that to the app. Existing support flows required users to already know what setting to adjust, which most did not.

The result was an experience that felt like talking to a wall. Users repeated themselves, gave up, or called support for problems the app could have resolved in under two minutes — if the content had been designed properly.

My Role

End-to-end content design, from first entry point to resolved outcome.

I owned the UX content design across the entire assistant experience — not just writing copy, but structuring the logic. Deciding how the assistant responds to ambiguous input, where branching happens, and how to handle scenarios where no immediate solution exists.

UX WritingConversation Flow DesignMicrocopy and ToneInteraction LogicResponse Variant WritingEdge Case Handling

The Work

Three connected outputs — onboarding, conversation flow, and response system.

The screens below show the home screen I wrote and structured: a clear assistant introduction, a prominent entry point, and five personalised suggestion prompts that give users a starting point without requiring them to know what to type. The assessment state on the right shows the open input experience — simple, conversational, with a voice option alongside text.

Brand names redacted

Left: Home screen with personalised suggestion prompts — designed to reduce the cognitive load of asking for help. Centre: Empty assessment state awaiting user input. Right: Active keyboard input state showing a real user message.

The extended flow below shows the complete conversation arc: the user submits a problem in natural language, the assistant identifies the scenario, provides a structured response with two clear action options, and then guides the user through a fine-tuning process. The fine-tuning screens include a branch for directionality settings — a technically complex feature written in plain language with clear opt-out at every stage.

Brand names redacted

Complete multi-screen conversation flow: problem identification, structured response with CTAs, fine-tuning initiation, directionality branch, and outcome confirmation. Each screen annotation shows my content decisions.

Behind the conversation sat a structured response matrix — built in Figma and shown below. For each of four ML-detected voice conditions (Own Voice too loud, too soft, unnatural, nasal), I wrote six distinct response types: solution confirmation, alternative solution, no-solution redirect, further iteration, solution kept, and issue inquiry. This ensured every user scenario had a considered, consistent answer rather than a generic fallback.

Brand names redacted

Response matrix mapping six response types across four detected voice conditions. Each cell is a distinct piece of copy calibrated to the specific user scenario — written to feed directly into the AI model response logic.

Key Decisions

Every content choice had a reason behind it.

Why I added suggestion prompts on the home screen

A blank input field is a barrier, not an invitation

Older users unfamiliar with AI assistants often experience blank text boxes as intimidating. Personalised suggestions — "First week with hearing aids", "Streaming issues" — lowered the barrier of entry and gave users a mental model of what the assistant could help with before they typed a single word.

Why I simplified all technical language

Users are not audiologists

Terms like "directional microphone mode" were replaced with descriptions of the user experience: "sounds coming from in front of you." The goal was to match the vocabulary of the user, not the product team. If a user could not recognise themselves in the copy, the copy was not working.

Why I built a structured response matrix

Isolated copy creates inconsistency at scale

Rather than writing each response separately, I created a matrix treating responses as a system. Any change to tone or terminology could be applied consistently across all conditions and response types. The development team also had a clear, maintainable reference rather than scattered copy documents.

Why every fine-tuning screen has a "Not now" option

Autonomy is part of good UX writing

Fine-tuning hearing aids is deeply personal. Removing the ability to decline at any step would increase pressure and reduce trust. A visible, non-judgemental exit at each stage — "Not now" alongside the primary CTA — respected user autonomy and kept the experience feeling supportive rather than pushy.

The assistant needed to feel like a knowledgeable friend, not a technical manual. Every word was a choice between sounding capable and sounding human. The goal was always both.

Outcome

A scalable content system, not just a set of screens.

The work produced was not just copy for a set of screens. It was a documented content system: conversation flows, a response matrix, tone guidelines, and a structured approach to edge cases — giving the team a reusable framework they could extend as new hearing conditions and scenarios were added to the product.

4ML voice conditions each with a full response set
6Response types per condition from solution to redirect
1Unified content system replacing isolated copy decisions
